segments.s473-g65: همه روی گیتی is a noun phrase meaning 'the whole face/surface of the world' with همه as a distributive/totalizing modifier before the head (روی). پر از + noun is a common Persian pattern meaning 'full of ...' where از is the preposition linking the noun (داد) and شد is the past of شدن used here intransitively to mean 'became' or 'was filled with'. Note داد is a noun 'justice' (not a verb) and the word order is poetic but grammatical Persian.
segments.s473-g66: به هر جای ویرانی آباد شد is locative: به introduces the location ('in/at/into'), هر جای = 'every place', with ویرانی qualifying جای ('places of ruin' or 'ruins'). آباد شد uses آباد (prosperous/inhabited) plus شد (past of شدن) meaning 'became prosperous' or 'was made inhabited'. The phrase thus states that ruined places were restored.
segments.s473-g41: ز (z) is the poetic preposition 'from' and introduces a source or location ('from the iwan'). The noun phrase ایوان (iwan/porch) follows the preposition. پرستندگان is the grammatical subject ('the worshippers/devotees') and خواستند is a third-person plural past verb 'they wanted/asked' (past simple); in context it introduces an action performed by that subject. Word order here is poetic but straightforward: prepositional phrase, subject, verb.
segments.s473-g42: چهل is the numeral 'forty' placed before the noun مهد ('cradle') as in regular Persian. زرین is an adjective 'golden' modifying مهد; Persian allows the adjective after the noun here for poetic effect. بیاراستند is the past 3rd-person-plural of the verb بی‌آراستن ('they adorned / they set in order'); it is the action clause that continues the narrative and shares (by parataxis) the same or a related subject from the previous clause.
segments.s473-g43: یکی here is the numeral/determiner 'one' meaning 'one (of the cradles)' rather than 'someone.' It directly modifies مهد ('cradle'). The preposition با 'with' introduces accompanying things: چتر is a canopy/cover (not the modern umbrella sense in this context) and خادمان are 'attendants/servants.' The repeated با before خادمان is emphatic/poetic and links each accompanier.
segments.s473-g44: نشست is the past 3rd-person singular of نشستن ('he/she/it sat' or 'was seated') — here a verb. Poetic word order places the verb first. اندرو is an adverbial phrase 'in it / inside it' (اند + رو = اندر + او), placed before the subject for emphasis. روشنک is the subject (a woman's name) and شادمان is a predicate adjective 'joyful' describing her state. The whole clause reads: 'Roshanak, joyful, sat inside it.'
segments.s473-g45: ز is the poetic 'from.' کاخ is 'palace' and دلارای is an epithet meaning 'charming / beloved' modifying کاخ. تا introduces the extent 'as far as' (here marking spatial extent) and نیم راه means 'half the way / halfway down the road' (نیم 'half' + راه 'road/way'). The whole phrase gives a spatial extent: 'from the charming palace to half the road.'
segments.s473-g46: درم is 'dirham(s)' (a coin) and بود is the past-tense copula/existential 'there was / there were' (past). The repeated و 'and' links a list of items: دینار (dinar/coin), اسپ (steed/horse), and سپاه (army/host). Grammatically, بود governs the whole coordinated list, so the meaning is existential: 'there were dirhams and dinars and horses and troops.'
segments.s473-g47: ببستند is a past 3rd-person-plural verb meaning 'they bound/tied/fastened' (here used with آذین 'decoration' to mean 'they hung or tied up decorations'). آذین is the direct object 'adornments/ornaments.' The preposition به 'to' marks the target, شهر is 'city,' and اندرون specifies 'the inner parts / interior.' The clause expresses a deliberate action of decorating the city's interior.
segments.s473-g48: پر ... از is the fixed combination meaning 'full of' (پر 'full' + از 'of/from'). خنده لبها literally 'the laughter of the lips' = 'smiles/laughter on the lips.' The conjunction و links the next nominal phrase: دل پر ز خون where دل 'heart' is described by پر 'full' plus the older poetic preposition ز (ز = 'of/from') and خون 'blood'; syntactically this is parallel: 'full of the lips' laughter and hearts full of blood.' The juxtaposition is poetic (joyous outward appearance and inner bloodshed).
segments.s473-g9: صد + noun: the cardinal 'صد' (hundred) precedes the counted noun as in Classical Persian. اشتر here is the counted noun (camels) though the provided lemma senses did not include that sense. ز is the poetic shortened preposition (like از) meaning 'from'. گستردنیها is a plural noun formed with the plural suffix -ها (literally “the spreadable/laid-out things”), acting as the source from which the camels are taken. ببر is the second-person imperative of بردن 'to take/bring' (addressing an agent: “take!”) and applies to the whole preceding noun phrase.
segments.s473-g10: This clause repeats the quantity construction: صد اشتر 'a hundred camels'. ز هر گونه dība: ز 'from/of' + هر گونه 'every kind/type' governs the following noun. دیبا (brocade/silk) is the material specified; به زر is a prepositional phrase qualifying the brocade ('with/in gold' or 'of gold') where به functions as a preposition meaning 'by/with' or 'in' and زر = 'gold'. Together the phrase specifies 'a hundred camels from every kind of brocade with gold'.
segments.s473-g11: هم = 'also' or 'likewise' marking addition. از + گنج دینار = 'from the treasure (ganzh) in dinars' (دینار here = money/coins). چو before a number often functions as 'about/amounting to' or 'as' and introduces the numeral phrase 'سی هزار' (thirty thousand). The whole chunk means 'also from the treasure, about thirty thousand dinars'.
segments.s473-g12: به بدره درون کن is an instruction with the imperative کن (do!/put!) that takes درون (inside) as adverbial/place and به بدره ('to/for the moon‑faced/beautiful one') as its indirect object: 'put (them) into/onto the beauty'. ز بهر is a fixed prepositional sequence meaning 'for the sake of/for' and نثار is the noun 'offering/dedication' — so the clause means 'put (them) to the beauty as an offering.'
segments.s473-g13: ز رومی کنیزک = 'from the Roman (Byzantine) maidservant(s)' where ز = 'from/of', رومی = 'Roman/Byzantine' as an adjective, and کنیزک is a diminutive noun 'little maid/maidservant'. چو + numeral (چو سیصد) here introduces the number 'three hundred' (چو functions roughly like 'about/as' before the numeral). ببر is again the imperative 'take!'.
segments.s473-g14: دگر here = 'the other/remaining'; هرچ = 'whatever/anything' (indefinite relativizer); باید is the modal 'must/should' (it is necessary); همه 'all/everyone' and the reduplicated سر به سر is an idiomatic intensifier meaning roughly 'one and all / completely / altogether' (literally 'head to head'). The sequence says: 'For the rest, whatever is needed must be given to all, altogether.'
segments.s473-g15: یکی جام زر = 'one cup of gold' (یکی = 'one/a single', جام = 'cup', زر = 'gold'). هر یکی را = 'to each one' where هر = 'each', یکی = 'one/person', and را is the definite/object marker marking 'each one' as the object. به دست = 'into the hand' or 'in (their) hand' (به = 'to/for', دست = 'hand'). The whole segment gives the distributive instruction: 'one golden cup to each (put) into the hand.'
segments.s473-g16: بر آیین خوبان = 'according to the custom/rite of the خوبان (the beloveds/beauties/lovers)' where بر = 'upon/according to', آیین = 'custom/ceremony', خوبان = 'the beautiful/beloved people' (poetic plural). خسروپرست is a compound noun 'Khosrow‑worshipper' or more broadly 'a flatterer/devotee of Khosrow' describing the social/customary orientation defining the ceremony. Together: 'in the manner/custom of the lovers who adore Khosrow.'
segments.s473-g49: This clause lists the objects first and finishes with the past plural verb ریختند. بران is a prepositional/adverbial particle here meaning 'upon/over that (or them)'. چتر دیبا درم are coordinated objects ('canopies of brocade and dirhams') and ریختند is the 3rd-person plural past of ریختن meaning 'they spread/poured/scattered'. The overall order (adverbial → objects → verb) is typical of epic Persian.
segments.s473-g50: ز introduces a source/starting point and بر marks the surface/target; together here they frame مشک سارا ('Sara’s musk') as the material or source. همی is the archaic/poetic verbal particle used with the verb to give aspect/emphasis; بیختند is the past verb (here used in the sense 'sprinkled/anointed/sprayed') though the dictionary senses given do not match exactly, so the verb is being used poetically to mean 'they scattered/sprinkled (musk)'. Word order is source (ز) → target marker (بر) → noun phrase → aspectual particle → verb.
segments.s473-g51: چو begins a temporal clause 'when'. ماه is used figuratively as 'the beautiful one' (indexing the addressee/heroine). اندر آمد is the compound verbal phrase 'entered/came in'. به + مشکوی شاه means 'into/before the musk‑scented king' or 'before the king, who is described as musk‑scented' — به marks motion toward or location 'before/to'. The adjectival مشکوی (musk‑scented) modifies شاه.
segments.s473-g52: This is a main clause: subject سکندر (Alexander) + indirect object/goal بدو ('to her' / 'toward her') + verb کرد ('made/did') and the noun نگاه ('a look/glance') as the direct object. The construction کرد نگاه literally 'made a look' = 'cast a glance'. چندی is an adverbial meaning 'for a while' modifying the verb phrase.
segments.s473-g53: This short nominal string gives epithets: بران ('thereon/upon that') introduces the scene; برز و بالا are parallel adjectives meaning 'exalted/high/lofty'; آن خوب‌چهر is a demonstrative + epithet 'that fair‑faced one'. The sequence is descriptive and prepares for the reported speech that follows.
segments.s473-g54: تو گفتی literally 'you said' (گفتی is the past 2nd-person or perfect form used for reported speech). خرد (wisdom) appears as the first element of an object/infinitival idea; پروریدش is the verb with the enclitic object -ش 'him/it' and here denotes 'to raise/nurture/foster (him)'; به مهر is a prepositional phrase 'with love/affection' specifying manner. In context this is reported speech/advice: 'you said: foster him with wisdom and affection.' Note گفتی is used to introduce the quoted imperative/command.
segments.s473-g55: چو 'when' begins the temporal clause. مادرش is 'his mother' (مادر + possessive suffix ش). بر تخت زرین is a locative phrase 'upon the golden throne' where بر marks placement and زرین is an adjective 'golden'. نشاند is an archaic/past causative/putting verb meaning 'set (her) (down)/seated (her)'; here it means 'caused her to sit' (i.e., placed her on the throne).
segments.s473-g56: This main clause names the subject سکندر, followed by the adverbial بروبر 'face‑to‑face / directly'. همی is the poetic verbal particle adding durative/emphatic flavour. جان is the direct object 'life/soul', and فشاند is the past 3rd‑person singular of فشاندن meaning literally 'scattered/caused to gush out' and here used metaphorically 'poured out (his soul)', i.e. gave himself over or showed intense devotion. Word order is subject → adverb → aspectual particle → object → verb.
segments.s473-g57: نشستند is past, 3rd-person plural ('they sat'). یک هفته gives the duration ('for a week'). با او is a standard prepositional phrase 'with him'; به هم here (literal 'to/for + هم') functions as a set phrase meaning 'together' or 'in council with one another', so the whole unit means 'they sat together with him for a week.'
segments.s473-g58: همی is the classical/poetic imperfective prefix indicating repeated or continuous action in past narrative ('kept on / was continually'). رای زد is a light-verb construction: رای (opinion/decision) + زد (literally 'struck') = 'pronounced/gave judgment(s)'; شاه is the subject/title 'the king'. بر بیش و کم means 'concerning/with respect to more and less' (بر = 'on/upon/regarding').
segments.s473-g59: نبد is the archaic/poetic negative-existential (like نبود) used in past narrative and here combines with جز to mean 'there was nothing except'. بزرگی is an abstract noun 'greatness/grandeur'. The phrase therefore reads 'there was nothing except greatness'.
segments.s473-g60: This is a simple list of qualities: خردمندی (wisdom/prudence), و (and), شرم (here 'modesty' rather than shame), و (and), شایستگی (worthiness/fitness). These nouns complete the 'except' clause begun in the previous segment.
segments.s473-g61: ببردند is past, 3rd-person plural ('they took' / 'they carried away'). ز ایران is the poetic preposition 'from Iran'. فراوان qualifies نثار and here is adjectival/adverbial 'abundant' modifying the following noun; نثار is 'offerings' or 'tributes'. The clause means 'they took abundant tributes from Iran.'
segments.s473-g62: This is a noun phrase specifying the tributes: ز دینار = 'of dinars' (from/of coin), وز گوهر شاهوار = 'and of gems fit for a king' — وز here appears as an archaic or fused form (likely 'و ز' = 'and of'), گوهر = 'gems', شاهوار = 'regal/lofty'. The whole gives the items taken as dinars and royal jewels.
segments.s473-g63: همه شهر is 'all the cities/towns' (a collective), followed by the place names ایران و توران و چین ('Iran and Turan and China'). This is a listing of the regions whose cities are meant; word order is typical: quantifier + noun + list of places.
segments.s473-g64: به شاهی = 'to kingship' or 'as king' (به + abstract noun شاهی = to the state/office of king). خواندند is past plural 'they recited/uttered/called out'; آفرین is the acclamation 'bravo' or 'well done'. برو in the given list is problematic in isolation (literal senses 'go'), but here it appears as part of the idiomatic sequence 'به شاهی برو خواندند' meaning roughly 'they proclaimed/raised to kingship' — خواندند is the main verb and آفرین is the shouted word.
segments.s473-g17: ابا here is a poetic/archaic form functioning as a contraction of او با (“he, with”); it introduces the person who moves with companions. خویشتن is emphatic/reflexive 'himself' placed after the particle to mark accompaniment ('with himself' = 'with him'). خادمان is the plural noun 'attendants/servants' and is the direct group accompanying him. بر + براه is a small prepositional phrase: بر (on/upon) + براه (an older spelling/compounding of به راه = 'the road, en route'), so the phrase means 'on the road' or 'while travelling.' Word order is typical older Persian: topic/agent (ابا خویشتن خادمان) before the locative (بر براه).
segments.s473-g18: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and introduces two parallel sources: راه ('the road, route') and آیین ('custom/practice') joined by و. شاهان is the genitive/attributive plural 'kings' qualifying آیین, and مکاه is a proper name (the name of a people/place or dynasty). The phrase as a whole gives the manner/source: 'from the route and from the custom of the kings of Makah' = 'in the route and according to the customs of Makah's kings.'
segments.s473-g19: بشد is a narrative past/imperfective idiom meaning 'it happened/it came to pass' or simply 'appeared/was' in this context. مادر شاه = 'the king's mother' (noun + genitive). با is the preposition 'with' marking accompaniment, and ترجمان is 'interpreter/translator' (here plural is implied by the numeral that follows in the next segment). The clause thus introduces that the king's mother arrived accompanied by interpreters.
segments.s473-g20: ده is the numeral 'ten' modifying an understood plural of 'translator' from the previous segment (‘with ten translators’). از is the preposition 'from/out of' and introduces the source group: فیلسوفان ('philosophers'). شیرین‌زبان is a compound adjective 'sweet‑tongued' (eloquent, pleasant‑speaking) modifying فیلسوفان. The whole segment therefore reads 'ten from the sweet‑tongued philosophers.'
segments.s473-g21: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (classical/poetic). آمد is the past tense 'came'. به نزدیکی is a locative phrase 'to/into the vicinity (near)' and اصفهان names the place. Word order is the common older Persian pattern: temporal marker + verb + locative phrase.
segments.s473-g22: پذیره here functions as 'reception/receiving' and is used with شدندش (شدند + enclitic ش) to form an idiomatic clause meaning 'they received him' (شدند is plural 'they became/made' and the enclitic ش = 'him/it' marks the object). فراوان modifies مهان as an adjective 'many/abundant', and مهان = 'guests/visitors'. So syntactically the clause says 'many guests received him' or 'he was welcomed by many guests.'
segments.s473-g23: بیامد is the past tense 'came' (narrative). ز is the poetic 'from', introducing the source. ایوان is 'iwan/porch/hall'. دلارای is an adjective 'beloved' or 'dear' qualifying the iwan. پیش here means 'before/in front' (indicating position relative to someone), so the phrase reads 'he came from the beloved iwan before him/that lay ahead.' The order places the motion verb first, then the origin phrase, a typical epic narrative order.
segments.s473-g24: خود is reflexive/emphatic 'himself' (here marking the principal person), و is the coordinating 'and'. نامداران is a plural noun 'the renowned/illustrious ones' (i.e., nobles). به + آیین expresses 'in/according to the custom/manner', and خوی + ش together form خویش ('their own' / 'his own') where خوی is 'habit/disposition' and ش is the enclitic 'his/their' — together meaning 'their own manner/custom.' The clause therefore says the famed men acted 'in their own custom.'
segments.s473-g33: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and is repeated here before each coordinate item; پوشیدنی is an -نی adjective/nominal meaning 'that which is worn' or 'wearable/garment'; و links the two parallel nouns; گستردنی is a parallel -نی form meaning 'that which is spread' (e.g. hangings, carpets). The phrase lists kinds of objects using the same preposition for each item.
segments.s473-g34: Again ز introduces the next pair; افگندنی is an -نی form from افکندن, here meaning 'that which is cast or thrown' (i.e. throwable or to be thrown); و links to پراگندنی, another -نی adjective meaning 'that which is scattered' or 'dispersible'. The two items form a second parallel pair of objects/actions.
segments.s473-g35: ز = 'from'; اسپان is the plural 'horses/steeds'; تازی here is specifying type as 'Arabian (horse)'; به introduces the attribute 'with/ having' (classical Persian often uses به + adjective to mark adornment); زرین is the adjective 'golden'; ستام is used as a noun here (poetic/archaic) describing the golden trappings/ornament (listed senses offered are limited, but syntactically this is a noun modified by زرین). The whole is a prepositional phrase listing a kind of equipage.
segments.s473-g36: ز = 'from'; شمشیر = 'sword'; هندی qualifies it as 'Indian' (type/origin); به + زرین again marks the swords' golden scabbards (زرین = 'golden'); نیام = 'scabbard/sheath'. This is another parallel prepositional phrase enumerating armed equipment.
segments.s473-g37: ز = 'from'; خفتان is an abstract/collective verbal noun related to خفتن ('to sleep') and here denotes 'bedding' or 'sleeping-things/places'; و connects items; از خود literally 'from his own' (از + خود) meaning 'from his own possessions/people/part'; and برگستوان is a verbal noun/infinitive form from برگشتن 'to return' used here as a noun 'the returning/return' or 'those who returned'. The sequence continues the catalogue of things/people supplied.
segments.s473-g38: ز = 'from'; گوپال here reads as the common noun 'cowherd' (or a proper name in some contexts) and is listed as another source/item; و connects; ز introduces another item 'from the dagger(s)'; خنجر = 'dagger'; هندوان = 'the Indians' (genitive relationship implied: Indian daggers). The pattern remains an enumerative list introduced by repeated ز/از.
segments.s473-g39: The correlative چه ... چه ... functions like English 'whether ... or ...'; چه مایه here means 'what amount/what matter' (i.e. 'whatever there was'); بریده is a past-participial/ adjectival form 'cut' (the cut ones); چه ... از نابرید pairs the opposite: 'or the uncut' (نابرید = 'unbroken/intact'). The phrase contrasts cut and uncut items and sets up the following negative statement.
segments.s473-g40: کسی ... ندید is an existential negative: کسی here functions as 'no one' (because of the negated verb); در جهان = 'in the world'; بیشتر = 'more' (comparative); زان = 'of that/than that' (referring back to what was listed); ندید is the past tense 'saw' (negated by context to mean 'saw no more'). Together: 'No one in the world saw more of that.'
segments.s473-g1: ز عموریه is a prepositional phrase 'from Amorium' placed before the verb (z = 'from' in poetic register). مادرش را is the definite object (مادر + enclitic possessive ـش 'his/her') marked by را. بخواند is a third-person past/epic-form verb here meaning 'he called/summoned' (classical b‑prefixed past form common in epic Persian).
segments.s473-g2: چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when/after ... came' (چو = 'when', آمد = past 'came'). سخنهای دارا is a genitive construction 'the speeches/words of Dāra' (سخنهای = plural 'words/speeches', دارا here used as the possessor/name). براند is a past/epic verb meaning 'drove away' or 'drove off' (the object is implied from context).
segments.s473-g3: بدو گفت is an object pronoun + verb phrase 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it', گفت = past of گفتن 'said' — here used as the reporting verb). نزد دلارای شو is an imperative instruction 'be/stand near Delarâ' or 'go into Delarâ's presence' (نزد = 'in the presence of/near', دلارای = proper name or 'beloved', شو = imperative of شدن 'be / become' used here as 'be, go be').
segments.s473-g4: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'with kindness / in a good manner' (به + خوبی turns 'goodness' into 'well/kindly'). به پیوند گفتار نو strings together manner and content: به (preposition/marker) + پیوند ('bond/link, here 'joining' or 'woven connection') + گفتار نو ('new speech/words'), so the whole phrase means roughly 'with kindly, newly‑wrought words' — an instruction about how to speak. Word order is typical Persian: preposition + modifier + noun.
segments.s473-g5: به پرده درون روشنک را ببین is an imperative clause: ببین is the 2nd‑person imperative 'see/look'. به پرده درون = 'inside the curtain' (به + locative phrase), روشنک را is the definite direct object (name + را). The locative phrase precedes the object and verb for emphasis.
segments.s473-g6: چو دیدی is a temporal clause 'when you see / when you have seen' (چو = 'when', دیدی = 2nd‑person past/perfect 'you saw' used in the protasis). ز ما کن is an elliptical imperative 'do it for us' (ز = 'from/on behalf of', ما = 'us', کن = imperative 'do/make'). برو آفرین are two imperatives in sequence: برو 'go' and آفرین used as an interjection 'cry "Bravo!"' or command to give praise.
segments.s473-g7: ببر طوق is an imperative 'take/bring the collar' (ببر = imperative 'take!'). با یاره و گوشوار lists accompanying ornaments ('with yāreh and earring'); با is the instrumental/augmentative 'with', linking the additional items to the object. The sequence is a command to collect these jewelry pieces.
segments.s473-g8: یکی تاج پر گوهر شاهوار is a noun phrase describing a single crown: یکی 'one/a', تاج 'crown', پر گوهر 'full of gems' (پر used as an adjectival 'full (of)'), and شاهوار 'kingly/regal' as a final qualifying adjective. Adjectives and adjectival phrases follow the head noun in this style.
segments.s473-g25: به (preposition) introduces the location ('into/onto the antechamber'); دهلیز names that location. کردند is the past plural verb 'they did/made' and here takes نثار as its object; چنان is an intensifier 'so much/so many' that modifies نثار. The order places the verb before its object (poetic/neutral Persian order) and چنان scopes over نثار.
segments.s473-g26: که introduces a result clause ('such that/that'); بر چشم is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to the eye' or 'in appearance'; گنج درم is a coordinated noun phrase 'treasure and dirham(s)' serving as the logical subject; گشت is the past of گشتن used copularly 'became' and is followed by the predicative adjective خوار 'worthless/despised'. The adjective follows the verb, yielding 'became worthless.'
segments.s473-g27: به ایوان is a locative phrase 'to/on the iwan'; نشستند is a past-plural verb 'they sat' (here used with location); با marks accompaniment 'with' and رای‌زن is an agent noun 'one who gives counsel' (an adviser). The phrase means they took their seats on the iwan together with the adviser(s).
segments.s473-g28: همه نامداران is the subject 'all the renowned (men)'; شدند is the past plural of شدن used as a copula here 'became' / 'gathered into'; انجمن is the predicate nominal 'an assembly' — so the clause means they assembled as a council. Note the sequence Subject + شد(ند) + Predicate is a standard Persian predicative construction.
segments.s473-g29: دلارای is the proper name (the subject). برداشت here functions as a past action 'took up/assembled' (the provided sense list is nominal but context is verbal: 'she gathered/laid out'); چنان is the intensifier 'so much' and جهیز is the object 'trousseau/dowry goods.' The order is Subject + verb + quantified object.
segments.s473-g30: که introduces the resulting clause. شد is the past copula 'became.' در جهان is a wide-scope locative 'throughout the world.' روی بازار is a possessive/figurative phrase 'the face/appearance of the market' acting as the subject of شد, and تیز is the predicate adjective 'sharp/keen/brisk' describing that appearance — together: 'the market's face became brisk.'
segments.s473-g31: شتر در شتر is a formulaic/poetic repetition meaning 'camel after camel' (a caravan). رفت is the past 'went/traveled.' فرسنگها is an adverbial/durational/distance expression 'for many parasangs/for leagues.' The sequence expresses a long procession of camels moving over a great distance.
segments.s473-g32: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' introducing the cargo/materials. زرین 'golden' and سیمین 'silver' name precious metals; و is the coordinating 'and.' وز here functions as an archaic/poetic conjunction equivalent to 'and' linking رنگها ('colors/colored cloths/ornaments'), a use not listed in the provided senses. The whole phrase lists what the camels bore.

sentences.s473-s33: The whole face of the world was filled with justice; every ruined place became prosperous.
sentences.s473-s21: From the iwan the worshippers asked, and they set in order forty golden cradles.
sentences.s473-s22: One cradle, with a canopy and with attendants, — Roshanak sat inside it, joyful.
sentences.s473-s23: From the charming palace as far as half the road there were dirhams and dinars and horses and troops.
sentences.s473-s24: They fastened decorations to the inner city; (it was) full of the lips' laughter and hearts full of blood.
sentences.s473-s5: Take a hundred camels from the spreads; a hundred camels of every kind of brocade, with gold.
sentences.s473-s6: Also take thirty thousand dinars from the treasure; put them into the beauty (the moon‑faced one) for the offering.
sentences.s473-s7: From the Roman maidservants take three hundred; as for the rest, provide whatever is needed, all one and all.
sentences.s473-s8: A single golden cup for each — put one into every hand — according to the custom of the lovers who worship Khosrow.
sentences.s473-s25: They spread over them canopies of brocade and scattered dirhams; from Sara’s musk they likewise sprinkled (her) with fragrance.
sentences.s473-s26: When the moon (the beauty) came in before the musk‑scented king, Alexander cast a look toward her for a while.
sentences.s473-s27: Upon that, the exalted and lofty, that fair‑faced one — you (then) said, 'Nurture him with wisdom and love.'
sentences.s473-s28: When his mother set her upon the golden throne, Alexander, face to face, poured out his soul.
sentences.s473-s29: They sat together with him for a week; the king kept giving his judgments about more and less.
sentences.s473-s30: There was nothing except greatness and gentleness.
sentences.s473-s31: They took from Iran abundant offerings — dinars and regal gems.
sentences.s473-s32: All the cities of Iran, Turan, and China acclaimed him as king, shouting 'Bravo!'.
sentences.s473-s9: He set out with his attendants on the road, following the route and the customs of the kings of Makah.
sentences.s473-s10: The king's mother appeared accompanied by ten interpreters from the sweet‑tongued philosophers.
sentences.s473-s11: When he came near Isfahan, many guests received him.
sentences.s473-s12: He came out from the beloved iwan before him, and the renowned men in their own custom.
sentences.s473-s17: From things to be worn and things to be spread, from things to be thrown and to be scattered.
sentences.s473-s18: From Arabian steeds with golden trappings, from Indian swords with golden scabbards.
sentences.s473-s19: From bedding (sleeping-places) and from his own (possessions) and from returns, from the cowherd and from the Indians' daggers.
sentences.s473-s20: Whether cut or uncut, no one in the world saw more of it.
sentences.s473-s1: He summoned his mother from Amorium; when Dara's words arrived, he drove her away.
sentences.s473-s2: He said to him: 'Go to Delarâ's presence; with kindness and a newly‑woven speech...'
sentences.s473-s3: Look at Roshanak within the curtain; when you have seen her, do it for us — go and cry 'Bravo!'
sentences.s473-s4: Take (bring) a collar with its pendant and an earring; also a single crown, full of gems, fit for a king.
sentences.s473-s13: They heaped offerings in the antechamber so lavishly that treasure and coins appeared worthless to the eye.
sentences.s473-s14: They sat on the iwan with an adviser; all the renowned men formed an assembly.
sentences.s473-s15: Delarai amassed such a trousseau that markets throughout the world became brisk.
sentences.s473-s16: Camels upon camels traveled for leagues, carrying gold and silver and dazzling colors.
